risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The statistician, who was blinded to the characteristics of the sites and the elders, then randomly allocated each site to either the TC or the control arm." Comment: The study explicitly states that sites were randomly allocated, and the allocation was performed by a statistician. Although the exact random sequence method (e.g., computer-generated list) is not specified, this provides sufficient context to support that a proper random allocation process was used in this cluster-randomized trial.

Quote: "Research assistants (RAs) recruited participants at each site and screened potential participants for eligibility. Assessor 1 conducted a pre-test for the outcome measures. The statistician, who was blinded to the characteristics of the sites and the elders, then randomly allocated each site to either the TC or the control arm." Comment: This was a cluster-randomized trial, and the report explicitly indicates that participant recruitment, eligibility screening, and baseline assessment occurred before the sites were allocated. Because enrollment happened before cluster assignment, the person enrolling participants could not foresee the upcoming group assignment for those participants.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The two lead investigators on the study (Tsai and Chang) were involved closely in the fieldwork and thus were not blinded to participants’ group assignments. The RA who screened elders for eligibility, enrolled the elders, and collected data on site also could not be blinded to group assignment. The same instructors led both the TC and the control groups so they were not blinded." Comment: Key study personnel delivering/administering the intervention were explicitly unblinded. Participants also would have known assignment because the interventions were obviously different (Tai Chi exercise vs instructor-led education control). The main outcomes included pain reports (VDS) and pain-related behaviors/analgesic use; pain outcomes are plausibly influenced by knowledge of allocation, so lack of blinding could affect outcomes.

Quote: "Assessor 1, who collected outcome data, could not be completely blinded because cognitively impaired participants revealed their group assignment during conversations with the assessor." and "The VDS (37), evaluated by Assessor 1... Elders with cognitive impairment were able to complete the VDS..." Comment: Outcome assessors included patient self-report (participants reporting VDS pain to Assessor 1), clinician/research-assistant collection by Assessor 1, a blinded coder for videotaped pain behavior ("Assessor 2, who reviewed and coded pain behavior, were blinded to group assignment"), and Assessor 1 for analgesic intake. By the worst-outcome rule, the study includes a subjective outcome most vulnerable to detection bias: pain self-report (VDS; and the paper also notes prior WOMAC verbal pain reporting in the parent trial). Participants were not blinded to allocation, and Assessor 1 was not fully blinded. Therefore knowledge of allocation could bias measurement of these subjective pain outcomes, making the overall domain High risk despite blinded coding of pain behavior and more objective pill counts.

Quote: "Data were also compared for participants who completed the study and those who dropped out. Missing data were imputed by the last observation carried forward; that is, the missing final values of the outcome were replaced by the last known value before the participant dropped out. An intent-to-treat analysis was used." Also, "[The CONSORT flow diagram was published online at http://www.sciencedirect.com/ as supplementary data.]" Comment: The paper confirms that some participants dropped out and that LOCF was used, but it does not report the actual post-randomization attrition numbers for the analysed outcomes at the relevant time point (e.g. per-arm randomized vs analysed at week 21), nor per-arm reasons for loss in the supplied text. Table 2 shows group sizes as Tai Chi n=28 and Control n=27, but this does not establish how many had missing outcome data by arm because imputation was used. Without reported per-arm attrition on the outcomes of interest, risk of attrition bias cannot be judged confidently as low or high.
